perturbation.original_text: The Prospective Vendor shall hold in confidence, and shall not disclose to any person or entity, any Proprietary Information. The Prospective Vendor shall use such Proprietary Infor mation only for the purpose for which it was disclosed and shall not use or exploit such Pro prietary Information for his own benefit or the benefit of another without the prior written consent of the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: The Prospective Vendor shall endeavor to hold in confidence, and shall not disclose to any person or entity, any Proprietary Information. The Prospective Vendor shall use such Proprietary Infor mation only for the purpose for which it was disclosed and shall not use or exploit such Pro prietary Information for his own benefit or the benefit of another without the prior written consent of the Company.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall hold' to 'shall endeavor to hold' weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality. While the vendor shall endevaor to hold, it doesn't mean it is obligated. This creates a conflict with Section 3, where it then states 'Without limitation of the foregoing, the Prospective Vendor shall not cause or permit reverse engineering of any Proprietary Information or decom pilation or disassembly of any software programs which are part of the Proprietary Information.'
perturbation.contradicted_text: Without limitation of the foregoing, the Prospective Vendor shall not cause or permit reverse engineering of any Proprietary Information or decom pilation or disassembly of any software programs which are part of the Proprietary Information. The Prospective Vendor shall be responsible to the Company for any disclos ure or misuse of Proprietary Information which results from a failure to comply with this provision.
perturbation.justification: The original text states the Prospective Vendor 'shall hold' proprietary information in confidence, creating a clear obligation. The changed text weakens this to 'shall endeavor to hold,' which does not create a strict obligation. This directly contradicts the Contradicted Text which states, 'The Prospective Vendor shall be responsible to the Company for any disclos ure or misuse of Proprietary Information which results from a failure to comply with this provision'. Because they only need to endeavor to hold and not be absolutely obligated, a failure to comply with this provision is less clear, and therefore there is a direct conflict regarding what obligations are owed.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations of the Prospective Vendor specif ied in Section 3 above shall not apply, and the Prospectiv e Vendor shall have no further obligations, with respect to any Proprietary Inform ation to the extent Prospective Vendor can demonstrate, by clear and convincing evidence, that such Proprietary Information. Page 2 of 3 (a) is generally known to the public at the time of disclosure or becomes generally known through no wrongful act on the part of the Pr ospective Vendor; (b) is in the Prospective Vendor’s possession at th e time of disclosure otherwise than as a result of Prospective Vendor’s breach of any legal obligation; (c) becomes known to the Prospective Vendor throug h disclosure by sources other than the Company having the legal right to disclose such Proprietary Information; (d) is independently developed by the Prospective Vendor without reference to or reliance upon the Proprietary Information; or (e) is required to be disclosed by the Prospective Vendor to comply with applicable laws or government regulations, provided that the Prospective Vendor provides prior written notice of such disclosure to the Comp any and takes reasonable and lawful actions to avoid and / or minimize the exten t of such disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of the Prospective Vendor specif ied in Section 3 above shall not apply, and the Prospectiv e Vendor shall have no further obligations, with respect to any Proprietary Inform ation to the extent Prospective Vendor states, without any need for evidence, that such Proprietary Information. Page 2 of 3 (a) is generally known to the public at the time of disclosure or becomes generally known through no wrongful act on the part of the Pr ospective Vendor; (b) is in the Prospective Vendor’s possession at th e time of disclosure otherwise than as a result of Prospective Vendor’s breach of any legal obligation; (c) becomes known to the Prospective Vendor throug h disclosure by sources other than the Company having the legal right to disclose such Proprietary Information; (d) is independently developed by the Prospective Vendor without reference to or reliance upon the Proprietary Information; or (e) is required to be disclosed by the Prospective Vendor to comply with applicable laws or government regulations, provided that the Prospective Vendor provides prior written notice of such disclosure to the Comp any and takes reasonable and lawful actions to avoid and / or minimize the exten t of such disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the need for 'clear and convincing evidence' and only requiring the 'Prospective Vendor states' that the Proprietary Information falls under one of the exceptions, the burden of proof is effectively eliminated. This creates a conflict with the intention of Section 4 and also makes Section 5 conflictive, which then states 'The Prospective Vendor agrees that the Company is and shall remain the exclusive owner of the Proprietary Information and all patent, copyright, trade secret, trademark and othe r intellectual property rights therein.'
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Prospective Vendor agrees that the Company is and shall remain the exclusive owner of the Proprietary Information and all patent, copyright, trade secret, trademark and othe r intellectual property rights therein. No license or conveyance of any such rights to the Pro spective Vendor is granted or implied under this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text requires 'clear and convincing evidence' for the Prospective Vendor to be relieved of obligations regarding Proprietary Information. The changed text eliminates this requirement, only needing the 'Prospective Vendor states' that the Proprietary Information falls under an exception, effectively removing the burden of proof. This contradicts the section stating 'The Prospective Vendor agrees that the Company is and shall remain the exclusive owner of the Proprietary Information', as the Vendor can now claim an exception without providing proof, undermining the Company's ownership.

perturbation.original_text: The Prospective Vendor shall, upon the terminati on of this Agreement or at the request of the Company, promptl y destroy or return to the Company all drawings, documents and other tangible manifest ations of Proprietary Information received by the Prospective vendor pursuant to this Agreement (and all copies and reproductions thereof).
perturbation.changed_text: The Prospective Vendor shall, upon the terminati on of this Agreement only, destroy or return to the Company all drawings, documents and other tangible manifest ations of Proprietary Information received by the Prospective vendor pursuant to this Agreement (and all copies and reproductions thereof).
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'or at the request of the Company' from Section 6 limits the Company's ability to request the return of Proprietary Information before the agreement's termination. This contradicts the intent of maintaining confidentiality throughout the period and after termination. This contradicts Section 3, which states that the information should be kept confidential. 'The Prospective Vendor shall hold in confidence, and shall not disclose to any person or entity, any Proprietary Information.'
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Prospective Vendor shall hold in confidence, and shall not disclose to any person or entity, any Proprietary Information. The Prospective Vendor shall use such Proprietary Infor mation only for the purpose for which it was disclosed and shall not use or exploit such Pro prietary Information for his own benefit or the benefit of another without the prior written consent of the Company.
perturbation.justification: The original text allowed the Company to request the return of Proprietary Information either upon termination of the agreement or at the Company's request. The changed text removes the 'or at the request of the Company' clause, limiting the return of information to only upon termination. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which states that 'The Prospective Vendor shall hold in confidence, and shall not disclose to any person or entity, any Proprietary Information,' implying a continuous obligation of confidentiality. By restricting the Company's ability to request the return of the information, the modified text weakens the confidentiality obligation outlined in the contradicted text.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall remain effective with respect to any Proprietary Information which is disclosed herein at any time with three (3) years f rom the date first dated above, except for the obligations of the parties hereto with respect to Proprietary Information received prior to such termination which shall survive such termin ation.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall remain effective with respect to any Proprietary Information which is disclosed herein at any time with three (3) months f rom the date first dated above, except for the obligations of the parties hereto with respect to Proprietary Information received prior to such termination which shall survive such termin ation.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the agreement effectiveness from three years to three months undermines the long-term confidentiality intended by the Company. This directly contradicts the section which states, 'The provisions of this Agreement are necessary for the protection of the business and goodwill of the Company'. Since the agreement lasts only three months now, it affects the statement that the agreement is for the protection of the business and goodwill of the company.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The provisions of this Agreement are necessary for the protection of the business and goodwill of the Company and are consid ered by the Prospective Vendor to be reasonable for such purpos e. The Prospective Vendor agrees that any breach of this Agreement wil l cause the Company substantial and irreparable damages and, therefore, in the event of any such breach, in addition to other remedies which may be available, the Company shall have the right to seek specific performance a nd other injunctive and equitable relief.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the effective period of the agreement from three years to three months. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which states that 'The provisions of this Agreement are necessary for the protection of the business and goodwill of the Company'. A three-month period is unlikely to adequately protect the company's business and goodwill, creating a strong and impactful contradiction. This is an example of Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction. The change alters the timing (duration) in a way that breaks consistency.
